PLAINTIFF AND DEFEN DAN' ON APPEAL FROM THE SUPREM# COURT OF VICTORIA. The Constitution, secs. 71, 73, 74—Appeal to High Court from judgment of Supreme Court of State—Judgment pronounced by single Judge in Chambers— Grier on originating summons —Conditions on Appeals — Special Leave— Judiciary Act 1903 (No. 6 of 1903) sec. 35—Supreme Court Act Victoria), (No. 112), oes. 8, 8, 37, 54, 55—Rules of Supreme Court of Victoria 1884, Or. LV.

The words "the Supreme Court of any State" in sec. 73 of the Constitution are used to designate that Court which at the time of the establishment of the Commonwealth was in any particular State known by the name of "the Supreme Court " of that State. Hild, therefore, that, subject to the conditions mentioned in that section, 'su ppeal lies to the High Court from every judgment &e. which, according to the law of a particular State, isa judgment &e, of the Supreme Court of that State, Saunders v. Borthistle, 1 UL.R., , followed.

Anorder made by a Judge of the Supreme Court of Victoria sitting in Chambers, upon an originating summons, by which the rights of the parties der will are finally decided is, under the Statute law of that State, an order of the Supreme Court, Senble, an order made in Chambers by a Judge of the Supreme Court of a Ste, even apart from express legislation in that State, is an order of the Sipreme Court of that State within the meaning of sec. 73 of the Constitution. The provision in sec. 73 of the Constitution that 'no exception or regulation Wesribed by the Parliament shall prevent the High Court from hearing or 'ermining any appeal from the Supreme Court of a State in any matter in H.C. oF A 190: Metnovrse, March 10, 13,April \ Grimith C.., Barton and O'Conner Id. 316 HIGH COURT H.C. or A. which at the establishment of the Commonwealth an uppeal lies. , 1905. the Queen in Councit" includes matters in which an appest then iy — with o without special leave of the Privy Council. oo The conditions imposed by sec. 35 of the Judiciary Act 1908 on appeals tat 'High Court from judgments &e. of the Supreme Court of a State aree *. ee Held, therefore, that no special leave is necessary to appeal from the — judgment of the Supreme Court of a State pronounced by a Judge it 'a Court of first instance for or in respect of any sum or matter at issue mo ing to, or of the value of, £300. Avpeat from the Supreme Court of Victoria.

An originating summons was taken out by Alfred Ernest Jane, one of the trustees and executors of the will of Charles Lister, deceased, to determine certain questions arising under such will 'The defendants were Annie Watson Lister, May Lister, Hi Lister, Elizabeth Parkin, Frank Cowper and George Lister, Edward Nathan Brown, assignee of the insolvent estate of Fra Lister. The summons was heard by Hodges J. in Chambers,and he made a certain order. From this order Elizabeth Parkin and Frank Cowper appealed to the High Court.

As this report only deals with a preliminary objection to the jurisdiction of the High Court to entertain the appeal, it is ut necessary to set out any of the facts of the case. MacArthur and Cussen, for the appellants. Higgins K.C., and Hogan, for the plaintiff respondent.
Trvine, for the defendants, A. W. Lister, May Lister and Hardll Lister, respondents, took a preliminary objection.

This Cout has no jurisdiction to entertain this appeal. Assuming that the decision in Saunders v. Borthistle (1), is correct, the order mall in this case is not an order of the Supreme Court within the meaning of sec. 73 of the Constitution.

Whether the Judge was exercising a part of the original jurisdiction vested in the Supreme Court, or whether he was exercising an additional jurisdiction given by the Rules under the State Act, his order is, by the terms of the Swpreme Cowrt Act 1890, expressly excluded (Q) 1C.L.R., 379. Where the Constitution says that the High Court may entertain appeals from judgments &c., of the Supreme "State laws is a judgment of the Supreme Court? To decide that qe must look at the Acts of the State and the Rules made under those Acts, There it is found that orders of the Court are to be chown up in a certain form, and that orders of a Judge are to be dawn up in another form ; this order is drawn up in the latter simi, and does not purport to be an order of the Supreme Court. The question is whether this is a judgment &eof the Supreme Court within sec. 73 of the Constitution. It does not affect that question that by a State Act or by Rules m under a State Act some judgments are for certain purposes call judgments of the Supreme Court, while others are called onles ofa Judge. Even looking at the Victorian Acts and Rules this. isa judgment of a Judge of the Supreme Court exercising the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court.

For it is under Order LY. 1. 8 of the Rules of the Supreme Court 1884 properly called a julgment, and see. 55 of the Supreme Court Act 1890 gives to a Judge sitting in Chambers power to exercise the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court. In Clover v. Adams (3) it was held that a Judge in Chambers was a Judge before whom the particular suit w depending, although the suit had never been before him and initiated in the Court. If, on the other hand, "Supreme Court" in the Consity tion means Court of final resort, it would be immaterial that in Victoria the local legislature has made the Courts of final re and of first instance one Court and called it the "Supreme Com 'The appeal would only lie from what is in effect the Court Appeal. j (Baroy J.—The use of the words "Federal Supreme Court' in see 71 assists that argument. In Victoria the Criminal Court is part of the Supreme and if the first meaning is given to the words "Supreme Court" in see. 73, a judgment in a criminal prosecution would be withia that section and from all sentences of the Criminal Courts ther would be asof right an appeal to the High Court, which ea wasnot intended. No. 10, a similar limitation should be plaed on the words when used in the Constitution.

But that does mot follow, for the Order in Council was intended to give a right appeal for Victoria only, while the Constitution was intended to have a general application to all the States. [O'Connor J.—I understand your argument to be that the wer "Supreme" is adjectival. Grirrira C.J.—Or that the words "Supreme Court" are m words of designation.] Yes. As the Constitution is intended to be for all time words mean the Court which at any particular time is the Court.

The only other alternative is that, to determine the extent if the appellate jurisdiction of the High Court, one must read nto see. 73 not only the State Acts but the rules of practice in the different States. The effect might be to destroy the appellate jurisdiction of the High Court; the effect would be to make the tdancter of that jurisdiction vary according to the varying and ot uniform legislation of each State.

So far as this particular tase is concerned it does not matter which of the two meanings isgiven to the words; because if it means the Supreme Court as constituted by the State laws, this order was not an order of that (Grrrita C.J—1f that were the meaning, this Court would be " sdomestie Court of appeal for every State, which would bea vast -funetion for the Court to have. 'The word "other" in the phrase "any other Court of any State fom which at the establishment of the Commonwealth an appeal its to the Queen in Council," is very important. Its effect is that the words "Supreme Court" mean the Supreme Court from which an appeal Ties to the Privy Couneil. 'The next question then is, at the establishment of the Commonwealth did an appeal lisfom a single Judge to the Privy Council? If there is any doubt as to the meaning of the words "from which... . an 'typeal ies to the Queen in Council," sec. 74 shows what is meant.

There can be no appeal to the Privy Council unless an Order in inl says that it lies, or unless special leave is granted. The Pramble to 7 & 8 Vict. c. 69 states that its intention is to enable # Queen in Council to provide for the admission of appeals from uts other than Courts of error or appeal. Under "se, 13 of the Constitution this Court is to have unlimited juristo hear appeals from certain specified Courts, subject only "toany exceptions of a certain kind that Parliament may make. (Git C.J.—The clause means that no subject matter of can be excluded altogether, but it does not prevent regula sas to the appealable amount for instance.] But an exception or regulation fixing the appealable amount at '41000 would transgress against the clause, because an appeal lies 'othe Privy Council where the amount exceeds £500. _Llrvine—That is not an "exception or regulation," but one of well known " conditions of and restrictions on" appeals to the ty Council referred to in the third clause of sec. 73.] (Cater C.J—That clause may mean that an appeal from le decision of a single Judge to the High Court shall be subject oun, H.C. or A. 1905. — PARKIN AND Cowrer JAMES AND Orns. 326 HIGH COURT H.C. or A. to the same conditions and restrictions as those applying 1005. appeals from a single Judge to the Privy Couneil.] Pawsis aso _ This Court is given a very large jurisdiction as to appeals Cowrsn it is left to Parliament to say to what extent it shall be eut » 'The second clause puts certain limitations on that Tastes Omens, right to the Privy Council, it would, as to the Supreme take away altogether the power to impose limitations on to the High Court. aa As to the term "appeal as of right," see Stafford and Whel on the Practice of the Privy Council, p. 708, where the modes of appealing are called "appeals by right of grant" al "appeals by special leave." In the third clause of sec. 74 distinction is clearly drawn between an appeal lying to the Couneil, and an appeal for which special leave must b to appeal. It is hard to see why a judgment of a single J sitting in Court is not a judgment of the Supreme Court wit the meaning of section 73. 'The section does not say thie matter?) It apparently would. This Court ought not to consider the present ineonvenience arising from a particular interpretation d the Constitution.

But it is of the highest moment that this Court should not put such an interpretation on the Constitution as denude itself of some jurisdiction apparently conferred upon it To interpret sec. 73 in the way suggested would be to limit th power not only of this Court but also of Parliament, for ment will be denied the power to legislate as to appeals from single Judge. This is an appeal from a decision of Hodges': in Chambers upon the hearing of an originating summons by whi the rights of the parties under a will were finally determined. preliminary objection was taken to the hearing of the appetl the ground that a decision of a Judge in Chambers on an 0 ating summons, although having the effect of a final adjudie of the rights of the parties, is not a judgment or order oft Supreme Court of Victoria within the meaning of the Const (1) 138.4, LR, 185. (2) 17 S.A. LR.y 110. OF AUSTRALIA. 20LR] tim We asked for further argument on the question whether in aayeae « judgment of a single Judge of the Supreme Court edng as a Court of first instance, from which an appeal Ties to the Full Court of the State, is such a judgment, and we are much sulebted to counsel for the assistance which they have given us.

We will deal first with the larger aspect of the question, which is 'one of great importance to litigants, not only in Vietoria but "throughout the Commonwealth, and might, if decided in favour of the appellant, have the effect of casting an unexpected burden of york upon this Court. 'This, however, cannot of course in any vay influence our decision. 'The appellate jurisdiction of the High Court is conferred by the Costitution, not by the Judiciary Act. Sec. 73 of the former provides that the High Court shall have jurisdiction "with such -cateptions and subject to such regulations as the Parliament pre~ ses to hear and determine appeals from all judgments decrees onlersand sentences" of federal Courts, or "of the Supreme Court of ay State, or of any other Court of any State from which at the tablishment of the Commonwealth an appeal lies to the Queen in Council," and proceeds : " But no exception or regulation presribed by the Parliament shall prevent the High Court from Iearing and determining any appeal from the Supreme Court of 4 State in any matter in which at the establishment of the Commonwealth an appeal lies . . . to the Queen in Council. Until the Parliament otherwise provides, the conditions of and ttrietions on appeals to the Queen in Council from the Supreme Courts of the several States shall be applicable to appeals from them to the High Cour Tt was suggested that the term "the Supreme Court of any State" was capable of meaning the Court of ultimate appeal in the State, as distinguished from the Court actually designated by 'that name, in other words, that the word " Supreme" is used as 'madjective of quality and not of designation ; and it is pointed tut that in sec. 71 the High Court is called a Federal Supreme Court, We all know that at the time of the establishment of the Commonwealth the designation of the highest Court of Judicature Heath State was the "Supreme Court," and that appeals then 'tyfrom those Courts to the Queen in Council.

If the suggested H.C. oF A. 1905, ~ PARKIN AND CowrEr Janes AND Ornens, H.C. or A. 1905. — PARKIN AND 'CowrER Tantes ax Omens. HIGH COURT meaning were accepted, the mention of the Supreme Cou ym would be unnecessary, since the words "any . . Count of State from which . . . . an appeal lies," would havi sufficient to include the Supreme Court. 'These words ate hy ever, clearly used to designate Courts other than the Supreme.

Court, and as distinguished from it. It is a matter of eommm knowledge that the immediate purpose of their insertion yyas ty inelude the appellate tribunal of South Australia consisting ofthe Governor in Council, although it by no means follows thatin their application they are confined to that tribunal, Applying, then, the ordinary eanons of construction, we cannot entertain any doubt that the term " Supreme Court" is used in the Coni tution to designate the Courts which at the time of the establ ment of the Commonwealth were known by that name. Témy be that the term would also include Courts established unde another name in substitution for them, but with similar function An appeal to the High Court is, therefore, given from all ju ments, deerees, orders and sentences of the Supreme Courts, bt, until the Parliament otherwise provided, the existing conditions and restrictions on appeals to the Queen in Couneil were tobe applicable to such appeals, while Parliament had power to make exceptions from the right of appeal, and to preseribe regulations as to its exereise, subject to the condition that the power of ths Court to hear and determine an appeal, in any matter in whichan appeal then lay to the Sovereign in Council, should not bedeniel.

It will be convenient to consider : (1)What was the law apple able to appeals from the Courts of a State to the Sovereign it Council at the time of the establishment of the Commonwealth! HIGH COURT iu See, 36 provides that the Full Court (which means all the Jug or not less than any three of them, see. 3) shall hear and determin all appeals from a single Judge, whether sitting in Court op Chambers. Sec. 87 empowers a single Judge sitting in subject to appeal in civil or mixed matters to the Full Court, to hear and determine all motions, causes, actions, matters, and proceedings not required under any Act or Rule of Court to be heard and determined by the Full Court.

See. 54 provides that any Judge of the Court sitting for the trial of causes and issues shall be deemed to constitute the Court. Pausing here, we are unable to see any substantial difference between the provisions of see. 31 and those of the Act 19 Viet. No. 13, already referred to, exept 's in which a single Judge exercises the powers of the Court, while those of the earlier Act were limited to the ease of a Judge exercising the equitable jurisdiction of the Court.

The fact that an appeal lay to the Full Court is common to judgments under both Statutes. See. 55 which is, with the exception of one word, practically a transcript of see. 39 of the English Judicature Act of 1873, provides that any Judge of the Court may, subject to any Rules of Court, exer cise in Court or in Chambers all the jurisdiction vested in the Court in all such causes or matters and in all such proceedings in any causes or matters as before the passing of the Judicature Act 1883, might have been heard in Court or in Chambers respectively by a single Judge, or as may be directel or authorized to be so heard by any Rules of Court to be here after made or for the time being in force. It concludes with the words " in all such eases any Judge sitting in Court shall be deemed to constitute the Court." The corresponding English section says "shall be deemed to constitute a Court," words which were obviously used to signify that a single Judge sitting in Court should have all the powers conferred on the Court as distinguished from those conferred on a Judge sitting in Chambers.

If the words of the Victorian Statute do not bear the same meaning, it is difficult to assign a definite meaning to them. There is nothing in the Act to suggest that they had any reference to a possible right of appeal to the Full Court, for an appeal is given by se 36 and 37 equally from all decisions of a single Judge, whether that the provisions of sec. 37 extend to all e OLR] OF AUSTRALIA. Rule T prescribes that the application shall be supported by such 'idenee as the Court or Judge may require, and that directions my be given as they or he may think just for the trial of any ae arising thereout. HIGH COURT tl 'These Rules, as to the validity of which no question is raise, manifestly made in execution of the power of the Court to Rules for initiating actions and proceedings in the Court term "action" isdefined by see. 3 of the Supreme Court Act 1899 to mean "a civil proceeding commenced by writ or in such off manner as may be prescribed by Rules of Court." A proce. ing by originating summons is manifestly a civil proceeding between parties which is commenced otherwise than by writ butin amanner prescribed by Rules of Court.

It is therefore an "action? and by r. 8 of Order LY. it is to result in a judgment, which how. ever, if made in Chambers, is drawn up as an order, There can be no doubt that the order so made, if not appealed from, has all the attributes of a judgment. The order may, but need not, be pronounced in Court. (Or. LIV. r. 9).

It finally determines the rights of the parties, it may be enforced in the same manner asa judgment (Or. XLIL r. 24), and it may be pleaded in estoppel ay ajudgment. The term "judgment" in the Constitution and in the Judiciary Act, as already pointed out, includes orders Reliance was, however, placed by the learned counsel for the respondent on the concluding words of sec. 55 of the Supreme Court Act 1890:—'Shall be deemed to be the Court," and on the diversity between these words and those of sec. 39 of the English Statute.

It was contended that the legislature of Victoria has by these words in effect declared that a Judge in Chambers is not the Court, and that consequently an order made by a Judge in Chambersis not an order of the Supreme Court. But, if a Judge sitting in Chambers is empowered to exercise and does exercise a juris tion vested only in the Court, how can his order be regarded otherwise than as an order of the Court? It has admittedly the effect of a final adjudication upon the rights of the parties ina controversy which can only be determined by the exercise of the jurisdiction of the Court.

If it is not the order of the Court from what tribunal does it emanate ? Is the Judge to be reganlél as an inferior tribunal, distinet from and subordinate to the Court? There is no doubt, as we have already shown, that a. appeal would lie from such an order to the Sovereign in Cound with leave, if not-without it.

But how? Would it be r@ i LR.) OF AUSTRALIA. anappeal from a subordinate tribunal, or as an appeal from "theSupreme Court? Can one suppose an appeal entitled "On sypal from a Judge of the Supreme Court of Vietoria?" We cannot see any satisfactory answer to these questions whieh yould admit the view that the order is not the order of the ont, The proceeding by originating summons introduced under the English Judicature Act by the Rules of 1883 was in sabstitution for the proceedings by summons in Chambers under the Act called Sir G.

Turner's Act (13 & 14 Vict. c. 35), as amended by 28 & 24 Vict. ©. 38s. 14, Under the former Act the decree was made on motion. So far as we know, it was never suggested that a decree of a Judge made on an originating summons under the old practice was in any way distinguishable in its quality or ects from any other administration decree. The only difference "yas that, if the hearing took place in Chambers, the disciplinary powers of the Court gud Court could not be exercised pending same incidents have always been held to attach to judgments pronounced upon originating summonses under the Judicature Adta Apart from the express provisions of the Victorian Statute, we "should feel great difficulty in holding that any order of a Judge jn Chambers is not in substance an order of the Court, within the meaning of the Constitution and the Judiciary Act.

In Iusl's Practice (2nd ed., by Stephens, p. 668) it is said that "the Common Law appears to vest in a single Judge, the same equitshlejurisdietion over the proceedings in a cause which it vests in the Court of which he is a constituent member. His act therein WS potentially the act of the Court; for, although he cannot thettly enforee the orders he makes nor exercise any of what may be termed the prerogative powers of the Court, yet the Gout will adopt his orders and for disobedience thereto when so opted will issue process of attachment as if the matter had been aigimally ordered by the Court itself." Accordingly, it has been il by Judges of great authority that when a Statute confers Povers upon the Court in general terms,and without any limitation Aer express or to be inferred from the context, they are to be "ered in the ordinary and usual way in which the Court is H.C. oF A. 1905. ~S PARKIN AND Cowper James AND Omirens. -thehearing. And, so far as we have been able to discover, the ~ James AND Orners. accustomed to exercise powers of an analogous nature, and that, it HIGH COURT it the powers in question are such as are ordinarily exercise jg Chambers, they may be so exercised: Smeeton v.Collier(1).

Tn su case the order is potentially the order of the Court. 'The powen of Judges in Chambers were confined within well known limits and technical rales prevented the enforeement of a Judge's order by execution or attachment until it had been made a Rule of Oot, but these rules are now abolished in Victoria, and there js a: already stated, no difference in the mode of enforcement between orders pronounced in Court and orders pronounced in Chambery It would indeed be strange if, although the effect of a inal as a final determination of the rights of parties is the same whether the Judge sits in an open or in a closed room, yet the right ofthe parties to appeal to this Court from the judgment should be dependent upon that cireumstance, which the legislature has in express terms declared to be immaterial for all other purposes, We are unable for these reasons to entertain any doubt that the order of Hodges J. under appeal is a judgment of the Supreme Court of Vietoria within the meaning of the Constitution and of see. 87 of the Judiciary Act. The objection is therefore overrule, and the case will remain on the list for hearing. Solicitors for appellants, Maddock & Jamieson, Melbourne.
